data.title: US begins probes into pharmaceutical, chip imports, setting stage for tariffs
data.description:
The Trump administration is kicking off investigations into imports of pharmaceuticals and semiconductors as part of a bid to impose tariffs on both sectors on national security grounds, notices posted to the Federal Register on Monday showed.

The filings set to be published on Wednesday set a 21-day deadline from that date for the submission of public comment on the issue and indicate the administration intends to pursue the levies under authority granted by Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962. Such section 232 probes need to be completed within 270 days after being announced.

The administration of President Donald Trump has started 232 investigations into imports of copper and lumber, and probes completed in Trump's first term formed the basis for tariffs rolled out since his return to the White House in January on steel and aluminum and on the auto industry.

The U.S. began collecting 10% tariffs on imports on April 5. Pharmaceuticals and semiconductors are exempt from those duties, but Trump has said they will face separate tariffs.

Trump said on Sunday he would be announcing a tariff rate on imported semiconductors over the next week, adding that there would be flexibility with some companies in the sector.

The U.S. relies heavily on chips imported from Taiwan, something then-President Joe Biden sought to reverse by granting billions in Chips Act awards to lure chipmakers to expand production in the United States.

The investigation announced on Monday will include both pharmaceuticals and pharmaceutical ingredients as well as other derivative products, the notice showed.

Drugmakers have argued that tariffs could increase the chance of shortages and reduce access for patients. Still, Trump has pushed for the fees, arguing that the U.S. needs more drug manufacturing so it does not have to rely on other countries for its supply of medicines.

Companies in the industry have lobbied Trump to phase in tariffs on imported pharmaceutical products in hopes of reducing the sting from the charges and to allow time to shift manufacturing.

Large drugmakers have global manufacturing footprints, mainly in the U.S., Europe and Asia, and moving more production to the U.S. involves a major commitment of resources and could take years.

data.title: Utilities Up as Traders Chase Momentum - Utilities Roundup
data.description:
Shares of power producers rose as traders chased momentum in the sector.

The SPDR Select Sector Utilities exchange-traded fund remains 1.9% higher for the year to date, despite bear-market conditions for much of the stock market.

"The fact that utilities are doing so well...maybe that's showing you further concern about getting into more defensive areas," said J.D. Joyce, president of Houston financial advisory Joyce Wealth Management. "It also could be the fact that demand seems to be increasing in the utility space, especially over the next five years or so."

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: Tech Up After Tariff Reprieve - Tech Roundup
data.description:
Shares of technology companies rose after President Donald Trump gave tech-hardware firms a reprieve on tariffs.

Electronics and computer makers would be temporarily exempted from tariffs, Mr. Trump said.

There's a risk of Amazon.com losing further ground on the stock market if tariffs kick in later, said one brokerage.

An Amazon selloff could transpire if there were "further deterioration in business confidence, labor market, and consumer spending; 2) Larger than expected first-party or third-party tariff costs or growth headwinds; and 3) Larger than expected investment to drive growth and take share," said strategists at brokerage Morgan Stanley, in a note to clients.

Shares of Apple, which had been under pressure because of its extensive manufacturing operations in China, rose by more than 2%, to cut losses for the year to date to roughly 17%.

Nvidia said it would start producing AI supercomputers that will be manufactured entirely in the U.S., a day after the Trump administration said tariffs are coming for semiconductor imports.

Self-driving truck startup Kodiak Robotics is merging with a blank-check company in a deal that values the company at about $2.5 billion.

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: Materials Up on Trade War Implications - Materials Roundup
data.description:
Producers of metals and other raw materials rose as traders bet the trade war would bolster commodities prices.

MP Materials shares surged after China banned exports of rare-earth minerals and magnets, widely used in electronics and electric cars. MP Materials owns a rare-earth mine and refinery in Mountain Pass, Calif., and is ramping up production of magnets derived from the rare-earth materials in Fort Worth, Texas.

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: Industrials Rise as Trump Delays Tech Tariffs - Industrials Roundup
data.description:
Shares of industrial and transportation companies rose as President Donald Trump delayed more tariffs.

The decision to postpone reciprocal tariffs on most countries was a short-term positive.

"History suggests that elevated and prolonged uncertainty that weighs on business confidence can have detrimental effects on business spending and hiring," said economists at brokerage Morgan Stanley. If bumper tariffs remain in place for some time, the economy is likely to slow to a crawl, said Federal Reserve governor Chris Waller, arguing the Fed would be compelled to cut interest rates as a result.

Volkwagen's Audi and Porsche brands are recalling thousands of vehicles in the U.S. due to various software problems.

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: Health Care Up on Defensive Demand - Health Care Roundup
data.description:
Health-care companies rose sharply as traders rotated into defensive sectors. Kidney-care firm DaVita said its operations were impeded by a recent ransomware attack.

Drug giant Pfizer halted development of its experimental weight-loss pill, as the pharmaceutical company struggles to find a follow-up to its success in the Covid-19 era.

Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly, weight-loss drug incumbents, rose sharply.

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: Financials Up After Goldman Earnings - Financials Roundup
data.description:
Shares of banks and other financial institutions rose as earnings continued to come in ahead of expectations.

Goldman Sachs Group rose after the investment bank said a surge in equities trading revenue fueled profit growth in the first quarter, which ended before the tariff-related market turmoil.

Exchange operator CME Group and index compiler S&P Global agreed to sell their joint exchange-services venture OSTTRA to funds managed by investment firm KKR for about $3.1 billion.

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: Consumer Cos Up After LVMH Report - Consumer Roundup
data.description:
Consumer companies rose slightly after a mixed report from a luxury giant.

Shares of Lous Vuitton owner LVMH inched higher after it reported first-quarter earnings that were modestly higher.

Even after a recent rebound, the consumer discretionary industry group of the broad S&P 500 is the biggest loser for the year to date, as confidence has evaporated in the face of tariffs and persistent inflation.

The rapid slide in stock portfolios has likely diminished confidence further, said one strategist.

"The wealth effect could come into play here as investors who have lost assets and whose net worth has gone down sometimes hold the purse strings a bit closer."

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: Communications Services Up on Defensive Demand - Communications Services Roundup
data.description:
Communications services companies rose, but not by as much as the broad market, as traders rotated into more defensive sectors.

Facebook owner Meta Platforms and the Federal Trade Commission squared off in a trial that could force the tech giant to break itself up by selling Instagram and WhatsApp.

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: Avarga Boosts Stake in Taiga Building Products to 74.2% with $10.1 Million Share Buy
data.description:
Taiga Building Products on Monday said Avarga , through its Avarga Canada unit, agreed to buy nearly 2.4-million shares of the company in a private transaction at C$4.20 per share, for a total of about C$10.1 million.

The deal was made under the private agreement exemption in Canadian securities rules. Before the transaction, Avarga already held about 72% of Taiga's shares. After the purchase, it will control roughly 74.2%, the company said.

Taiga shares closed down $0.05 to $3.69 on the Toronto Stock Exchange.

data.title: Salem Media Gets Investment from Donald Trump Jr.
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Donald Trump Jr. and Lara Trump are investing in the Christian and conservative media company Salem Media.

Salem said Monday it acquired a 30% stake in MxM News, a mobile news aggregation app co-owned by Trump Jr. It also entered into a services agreement to develop a series of promotional initiatives with the president's son.

Salem has also entered an agreement to work with the president's daughter-in-law Lara Trump to grow its digital podcast business and advertiser partnerships. She renewed her exclusive podcast agreement and will continue to produce and host a show on the Salem Podcast Network.

Both Lara and Donald Trump Jr. are becoming "significant" stakeholders in Salem, the company said.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: Boston Beer Is Maintained at Buy by Citigroup
Conduent Incurs Non-Recurring Expenses in Q1 due to Cyberattack
data.description:
Conduent has incurred material non-recurring expenses in Q1 linked to a cyber incident in January, the company said in a regulatory filing late Monday.

On Jan. 13, attackers gained access to a "limited portion" of Conduent's systems, the company said, adding it restored the systems and returned to "normal operations within days, and in some cases, hours."

The attackers extracted files related to some of Conduent's clients and some data contained a "significant number of individuals' personal information associated with [its] clients' end-users," the company said.

Conduent said its customers are being informed to determine the next steps, as required by law.

THE LAWSUIT: This lawsuit is on behalf of all shareholders that purchased stock pursuant and/or traceable to Venture's registration statement for the initial public offering held on or about January 24, 2025.

CASE DETAILS: According to the complaint, Venture completed its initial public offering on January 27, 2025, selling 70 million shares at $24.00 per share. On February 5, 2025, TotalEnergies, an energy company that was a target customer of Venture, rejected opportunities to become a long-term customer of Venture, citing lack of trust. In particular, TotalEnergies CEO, stated that he was approached by Venture to see if the company would be interested in a long-term supply contract for liquefied natural gas from the Calcasieu Pass terminal in Louisiana, but he rejected the offer "because of what they are doing."

Venture is currently facing legal challenges from existing large clients, such as BP and Shell, due to delays in supply contracts as Venture commissions its projects. Given the fact that defendants ability to deliver liquefied natural gas (LNG) to the world and to continue development of Venture's five natural gas liquefication and export projects depends on customer contracts, defendants' failure to account for and address these issues caused statements in Venture's registration statement to be false and/or materially misleading at the time of the initial public offering.

data.title: IRS chief information officer announces resignation on eve of tax-filing day
data.description:
The chief information officer at the Internal Revenue Service said he would resign, according to an email sent to staff and seen by Reuters on Monday, the day before the April 15 tax-filing deadline for most U.S. taxpayers.

CIO Rajiv Uppal, whose job was to oversee the development and improvement of the IRS' computer and IT systems, said his resignation from the tax agency would be effective late this. The move came as President Donald Trump's administration and tech billionaire Elon Musk's Department of Government Efficiency are seeking to cut thousands of IRS staff.

"After careful thought, I've decided to depart the IRS effective April 28, 2025," Uppal wrote to staff. He added, "I do understand this is a time of transition for many, and I want to thank you for your steady commitment through it all."

Members of Musk's cost-cutting DOGE team have been inside the IRS since February and have gained access to agency databases containing taxpayer information.

Uppal's departure will likely leave Kaschit Pandya, the chief technology officer, to play an even larger role managing the agency's IT systems, according to two people familiar with the matter. Pandya has been working closely with members of DOGE over the past two months, the people said.

Musk and Trump say they want to cut the cost and size of the federal bureaucracy and eliminate waste and fraud, including at the IRS.

Some former IRS officials and governance experts have warned that creating uncertainty and making cuts at the IRS during the height of tax-filing season could result in the loss of tax revenue into government coffers.

The IRS, the White House and DOGE did not immediately response to a request for comment.

Uppal did not mention the work of DOGE in his resignation letter.

data.title: Veralto Is Maintained at Neutral by Citigroup
Applied Materials Buys 9% Stake in Dutch BE Semiconductor Industries
data.description:
By Sabela Ojea

Applied Materials acquired a 9% stake in BE Semiconductor Industries after years of collaboration with the Dutch manufacturer of assembly equipment for the chip industry.

The Santa Clara, Calif., semiconductor-equipment company on Monday said the interest taken in BE Semiconductor Industries, also known as Besi, represents a strategic long-term investment and it doesn't intend to further increase its stake or seek board representation.

Applied added that the investment isn't subject to regulatory approvals, since it was made through market-based transactions.

The announcement comes as the market awaits President Trump's plans to impose tariffs on semiconductor companies. Nvidia on Monday morning said it would build its AI supercomputers entirely in the U.S. because of the tariffs.

Applied and Besi have been collaborating since 2020, and recently extended an agreement to co-develop what they called the industry's first fully integrated equipment solution for die-based hybrid bonding.

Hybrid bonding connects chips using direct copper-to-copper bonds, which increases density and shortens the lengths of interconnect wiring between chiplets, resulting in improved overall performance, power consumption and cost, Applied noted.

Write to Sabela Ojea at sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix

data.title: Hassett: 'I Don't See a Recession'
data.description:
By Joseph Pisani

Kevin Hassett, director of the National Economic Council, said he doesn't expect a recession, citing recent strong job numbers and talks he's had with company leaders.

"I don't see a recession at all," he said in a Fox Business interview Monday afternoon. Hassett said he's spoken to CEOs who say their orders are going well. "People are actually euphoric," he said.

Hassett also said the U.S. is close to making trade deals with more than 10 countries. "It's going up every day," he said.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Vontier Is Maintained at Buy by Citigroup
M2i Global, Inc. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
M2i Global, Inc., a company transitioning from mobile software applications to a global value supply chain for critical minerals, has released its Form 10-Q report for the first quarter of 2025. The report provides insights into the company's financial performance and significant business developments as it implements its new business model.

Financial Highlights

  • Revenue: $0, The company reported no revenue for the three months ended February 28, 2025, as it is currently pre-revenue and anticipates future revenues as it implements its new business model.
  • Loss from operations: $(1.34) million, The loss from operations increased significantly compared to the previous year, primarily due to an increase in professional fees.
  • Net Loss: $(1.35) million, The net loss for the three months ended February 28, 2025, increased compared to the same period in 2024, reflecting higher operating expenses.
  • Loss per share: $(0.00), The loss per share remained unchanged despite the increase in net loss, due to a higher number of shares outstanding.

Business Highlights

  • Business Model Shift: M2i Global, Inc. has transitioned from developing mobile software applications to specializing in the development and execution of a global value supply chain for critical minerals, focusing on three key divisions: M2i Mining, Processing & Refining, M2i Scrap & Recycling, and M2i Government and Defense Industrial Base.
  • Strategic Partnerships: The company entered into a strategic partnership with Komodo Capital to leverage its relationships for access to critical minerals, aiming to supply the U.S. government and free trade partners.
  • Exclusive Offtake Agreement: M2i Global, Inc. signed an exclusive offtake agreement with NTM Minerals Limited for 88,000 tonnes of copper, valued at approximately $850 million, in exchange for 12 million shares of the company's common stock.
  • Future Revenue Expectations: The company anticipates that revenues will begin to materialize in the first or second quarters of the next fiscal year as it implements its new business model.
  • Operational Expansion: The company expects significant capital expenditures to support the expansion of operations in line with its growth plans.
  • Cybersecurity Strategy: M2i Global, Inc. has integrated cybersecurity risk management into its broader risk management framework, emphasizing the importance of safeguarding information systems and managing third-party risks.

SEC Filing: M2i Global, Inc. [ MTWO ] - 10-Q - Apr. 14, 2025

data.title: Digital Brand Media & Marketing Group, Inc. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Digital Brand Media & Marketing Group, Inc., a company specializing in marketing consulting services with a focus on AI-driven solutions, has released its Form 10-Q report for the first quarter of 2025. The report highlights the company's financial performance and strategic business initiatives amid a challenging market environment.

Financial Highlights

Revenues: The company reported revenues of $27,604 for the three-month period ended February 28, 2025, a significant decrease from $80,784 in the same period in 2024, indicating a challenging market environment.

Gross Profit: Gross profit for the three-month period ended February 28, 2025, was $2,750, down from $15,745 in the same period in 2024, highlighting increased cost pressures.

Operating Loss: The operating loss for the three-month period ended February 28, 2025, was $(191,629), compared to $(85,860) in the same period in 2024, due to higher operating expenses.

Net Income (Loss): The company reported a net income of $184,504 for the three-month period ended February 28, 2025, compared to a net loss of $(189,452) in the same period in 2024, primarily due to a gain on derecognition of liabilities.

Net Income Per Share: Net income per share was $0.00 for the three-month period ended February 28, 2025, reflecting the impact of the gain on derecognition of liabilities.

Business Highlights

Geographical Performance: The company generates its income primarily from marketing consulting services provided in the United States and Great Britain. For the six-month period ended February 28, 2025, revenues were $7,755 from the United States and $48,822 from Great Britain. In the previous year, for the six-month period ended February 29, 2024, revenues were significantly higher with $107,883 from the United States and $58,451 from Great Britain, indicating a decline in revenue from both regions in 2025.

Future Outlook:

  • The company is focusing on expanding its presence in the US market, particularly in the B2B tech sector, with a strategic emphasis on AI-driven marketing solutions. This includes establishing a strong presence in tech hubs like Irvine, California.
  • Digital Clarity is pivoting towards AI and digital transformation, aiming to leverage AI for personalized marketing strategies and enhanced customer engagement. The company anticipates AI will become a necessity for businesses aiming to thrive in the digital economy by 2025.
  • The company plans to focus on aggressive client acquisition in high-growth sectors, with expectations of 35%-40% quarter-on-quarter revenue growth through consultancy services. This growth is expected to be sustainable with new client onboarding and the application of AI technologies.
  • The company is preparing for a significant expansion in its US footprint, with a focus on the tech sector, and anticipates a 36% growth in demand for marketing consulting services in North America by 2027.
  • Digital Clarity is positioning itself as a leader in AI-driven marketing, with plans to enhance its consultancy model and expand its service offerings to include AI-powered content creation and campaign management.
  • The company is committed to maintaining its competitive advantage by integrating AI into its marketing strategies, aiming to deliver personalized experiences and optimize marketing ROI for its clients.

SEC Filing: Digital Brand Media & Marketing Group, Inc. [ DBMM ] - 10-Q - Apr. 14, 2025

data.title: Wall Street ends higher with Apple shares as investors assess tariff exemptions
data.description:
Key points:
  • Apple shares gain on exemptions on tariffs
  • Goldman Sachs rises after results
  • More earnings to come this week including from Netflix
  • Indexes: Dow up 0.8%, S&P 500 up 0.8%, Nasdaq up 0.6%

U.S. stocks ended higher on Monday, with Apple giving the S&P 500 its biggest boost as the White House exempted smartphones and computers from new tariffs.

Uncertainty over future tariffs kept a lid on optimism, with the main indexes finishing off their highs of the day. Investors remain worried about how companies will manage supply chains as more changes are expected on the tariff front.

The United States unveiled the exemptions on Friday, but President Donald Trump said on Sunday that he would be announcing the tariff rate on imported semiconductors over the next week.

Global technology shares mostly rose on the news, especially for companies that rely on imports from China. Shares of iPhone maker Apple rose 2.2%. Dell Technologies gained 4% and HP climbed 2.5%.

At the same time, an index of semiconductors rose just 0.3% and shares of top chipmaker Nvidia were down 0.2% on the day.

Monday's trading was choppy, as has been the case since Trump announced sweeping tariffs on April 2. Investors, worried that a global trade war will push the economy into recession, have seen some of the biggest swings in the market in years as the Trump tariff news changes.

"Really what we have is just continued uncertainty and inability for consumers and businesses and investors to plan much going forward or have reason to commit to long-term spending plans," said Jed Ellerbroek, a portfolio manager at Argent Capital Advisors in St. Louis, Missouri.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 312.08 points, or 0.78%, to 40,524.79, the S&P 500 rose 42.61 points, or 0.79%, to 5,405.97 and the Nasdaq Composite rose 107.03 points, or 0.64%, to 16,831.48.

The CBOE Volatility Index , Wall Street's "fear gauge," eased to 30.89, its lowest closing level since April 3.

Technical analysts noted, though, that the S&P 500 is now in a "death cross" pattern, which marks a spot where a shorter-term correction could turn into a longer-term downtrend.

A death cross occurs when the 50-day moving average slips below the 200-day moving average.

History suggests the ominous-sounding signal may not necessarily mean equities face more significant downside. The S&P 500 remains down about 8% for the year so far.

Markets will be closed on Good Friday, but this week is still expected to bring some key results from U.S. companies.

U.S. companies have begun to report results for the 2025 first quarter and, with tariff troubles looming, corporate executives may hold back on giving much guidance.

"Everybody knows the future is going to look a fair amount different than the past, and management teams are going to be really hesitant to commit to much," Ellerbroek said.

Still, shares of Goldman Sachs rose 1.9% on Monday after the bank reported higher first-quarter profit. Quarterly results from companies including Netflix and UnitedHealth Group are also on the radar this week.

Also gaining were some drugmakers after Pfizer said it would end the development of its experimental weight-loss pill. Pfizer shares ended 1% higher.

On the Nasdaq, 3,266 stocks rose and 1,200 fell as advancing issues outnumbered decliners by about a 2.72-to-1 ratio. There were 43 new highs and 101 new lows.

On the NYSE, advancing issues outnumbered declining ones by a 4.4-to-1 ratio . There were 45 new highs and 65 new lows.

Volume on U.S. exchanges was 18.2 billion shares, compared with the roughly 18.7 billion average for the full session over the last 20 trading days.

data.title: SEC delays crypto ETF staking and in-kind redemption decisions as agency mulls long-term crypto regulatory strategy
data.description:
The U.S. Security and Exchange Commission has delayed its decision regarding staking ETFs as well as in-kind creations and redemptions for several cryptocurrency exchange-traded funds to later this year. The regulator intends to give itself more time to assess these rule changes as its in-house crypto task force considers how to implement long-term and comprehensive digital asset regulation. 

The crypto task force was formed to accelerate the creation of sensible guidelines and policies for the crypto industry. So far, the group has met with a number of industry players to gather their recommendations and has rolled back most of the previous administration's enforcement actions against crypto firms. In February, Jito and Multicoin Capital executives met with the SEC to advocate for staking ETFs, which could pay yield generated by locking up assets to investors. 

The first decision to be delayed involved the Grayscale Ethereum Trust ETF and Grayscale Ethereum Mini Trust ETF, two ether-focused investment products managed by Grayscale Investments. On Feb. 14, the stock exchange NYSE Arca requested a rule change to let the two trusts stake the held ETH, the native token for Ethereum. The SEC had until Apr. 17 to make a decision, but delayed it to June 1, according to an SEC document filed on Monday. 

Additionally, the Cboe BZX stock exchange proposed rule changes so that it could permit in-kind creations and redemptions for the VanEck Bitcoin Trust and the VanEck Ethereum Trust. In-kind creations and redemptions are when an investor uses the underlying asset, rather than cash, to create or redeem shares of a trust. In this case, those securities would have been the cryptocurrencies bitcoin and ethereum for VanEck's respective ETFs. 

Cboe BZX first proposed the rule change on Feb. 19, leaving the SEC had until Apr. 19 to make its decision to accept, deny or delay the rule changes, as noted in another SEC release. The regulator is now giving itself another 45 days to make that decision, aiming to make the final call on June 3. 

The SEC also postponed a decision regarding in-kind creation and redemption for the WisdomTree Bitcoin Fund to June 3. 

"The Commission finds it appropriate to designate a longer period within which to take action on the proposed rule change so that it has sufficient time to consider the proposed rule change and the issues raised therein," Sherry R. Haywood, assistant secretary to the SEC, wrote in a Monday document regarding VanEck's crypto ETFs. 

The VanEck Bitcoin Trust, with its ticker symbol HODL, and the VanEck Ethereum Trust are a spot bitcoin ETF and spot ether ETF, respectively, out of the New York City-based investment management firm VanEck. HODL brought in $19.58 million in volume on Apr. 11, The Block's Data Dashboard shows. 

Disclaimer: The Block is an independent media outlet that delivers news, research, and data. As of November 2023, Foresight Ventures is a majority investor of The Block. Foresight Ventures invests in other companies in the crypto space. Crypto exchange Bitget is an anchor LP for Foresight Ventures. The Block continues to operate independently to deliver objective, impactful, and timely information about the crypto industry. Here are our current financial disclosures.

© 2025 The Block. All Rights Reserved. This article is provided for informational purposes only. It is not offered or intended to be used as legal, tax, investment, financial, or other advice.

data.title: Bristol Myers falls as heart disease drug fails in late-stage trial
data.description:
** Shares of drugmaker Bristol Myers Squibb fall 1.6% to $50.50 in extended trading

** Co says its experimental heart disease drug, mavacamten, did not meet the main goals in a late-stage study

** The drug was being tested to treat adult patients with with the so-called non-obstructive form of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (nHCM), an inherited condition characterized by thickening of heart muscles

** The drug, branded as Camzyos, did not improve symptoms or exercise more easily in patients with nHCM

** The drug is already approved in the U.S. for the obstructive form of the disease

** As of last close, stock down 9.2% YTD
